content:
12 min read
1 hour ago

--

The behavior was not random. Our mental model of when callbacks, promises, timers, and state updates would run was wrong.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The bug looked random because the result changed depending on timing.
The event loop was not behaving randomly.
Our assumptions were.
Sometimes the older request won. Sometimes the loading spinner disappeared too early. Sometimes a timer ran after the interface had already changed. The same action could produce a different result when the network was slower.
Every log appeared correct in isolation.
Together, they told a story our code had never intended.
The problem was not that JavaScript ignored execution order. The problem was that we misunderstood which order JavaScript had actually promised.
Asynchronous code is easy to read as if it were sequential. One line starts a request. The next updates state. A callback appears below another callback, so we assume it will happen later. Two operations begin in a particular order, so we expect them to finish in that order.
Those expectations survive until real timing reaches the application.
A request takes longer than usual. A user clicks twice. A resolved promise runs before a timer. A component unmounts while work is still pending. An old callback reads state captured several renders ago.
The application then appears unpredictable, even though every part of the runtime is behaving consistently.
The event loop does not create randomness.
It exposes code that depends on an order nobody guaranteed.

Source Order Is Not Completion Order
Synchronous code encourages a reliable reading habit: move from top to bottom.
One statement completes before the next begins. The source order closely matches the execution order. That relationship becomes so familiar that developers continue applying it after asynchronous work enters the program.
Consider a familiar pattern:
setLoading(true);
fetchData().then(setData);
setLoading(false);The code reads like a three-step process:

• Start loading.

• Fetch the data.

• Stop loading.
That is not what the program does.
It turns loading on, starts the request, registers a callback, and immediately turns loading off. The callback runs later, after the request completes and the runtime is able to process it.
The mistake is not forgetting the syntax of promises. Most developers understand that .then() is asynchronous.
The mistake is reading the purpose of the code instead of the execution contract.
The intended workflow is sequential. The implementation is not.
This distinction becomes harder to see when asynchronous behavior is spread across functions:
async function refreshPage() {
startLoading();
loadUser();
loadNotifications();
stopLoading();
}The names imply a meaningful sequence. But if loadUser() and loadNotifications() start asynchronous work without returning promises that refreshPage() waits for, stopLoading() still runs immediately.
The code looks organized while ownership of completion is missing.
This is why event loop mistakes survive review. Each function appears reasonable on its own. The failure lives between them.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

practical guideReliable asynchronous code requires the caller to know what it owns.
Does calling this function mean the work has finished, or only that the work has started? Does it return a promise representing completion? Who handles failure? Who decides when loading ends? Can the operation still update state after the caller no longer cares about the result?
Without explicit answers, developers fill the gaps with source-order intuition.
That intuition works until timing changes.
The First Request Did Not Always Finish First
One of the most common timing failures begins with two valid requests.
A user selects one filter, then quickly selects another. The first request begins before the second. The second request finishes first.
The interface displays the correct result.
Then the first response arrives and overwrites it.
The screen now shows data for the old filter while the controls display the new one.
Nothing failed technically. Both requests succeeded. Both callbacks ran. The state update logic worked exactly as written.
The application failed because it assumed start order would determine completion order.
Networks make no such promise.
A small response can finish before a large one. A cached endpoint can return before an uncached request. One server instance may be under heavier load. A retry may delay an operation that began earlier.
The frontend often treats these variations as rare edge cases, but any user who changes input faster than the network responds can create overlapping timelines.
Search boxes are a common example. Each keystroke starts a request:

• j

• ja

• jav

• java
The request for java may return first. The interface shows the correct results. Then the slower request for ja finishes and replaces them.
Developers sometimes fix this with debouncing, which reduces request volume but does not fully solve ownership. Two requests can still overlap if the user pauses, types again, or the network behaves unpredictably.
The real question is not how many requests were started.
It is which response still has permission to update the interface.
That can be handled through cancellation, request identifiers, sequence numbers, or a data-fetching library that tracks stale requests. The exact mechanism matters less than the decision.
A response should not be accepted merely because it arrived.
It should be accepted because it still belongs to the current user intent.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

This principle applies beyond fetching data. Autosave, validation, file uploads, authentication refreshes, and background calculations can all finish in a different order from the one in which they started.
Asynchronous work needs an ownership rule.
Without one, the last callback to run becomes the accidental source of truth.
Promises and Timers Did Not Enter the Same Queue
Developers often use timers as a way to say, “Do this later.”
That description is directionally correct but too vague for code that depends on precise order.
A zero-millisecond timer does not run immediately. It schedules a callback to become eligible after the current work and higher-priority queued work have completed.
Promises follow different scheduling rules.
This creates output that can surprise developers who read only from top to bottom:
console.log("start");
setTimeout(() => {
console.log("timer");
}, 0);
Promise.resolve().then(() => {
console.log("promise");
});
console.log("end");The result is:
start
end
promise
timerThe promise callback runs before the timer callback, even though the timer was registered first.
This is often introduced as an event loop trivia question. In production code, it is not trivia.
The distinction matters when applications mix promise chains, timers, framework updates, observers, and event handlers while assuming that “later” means one predictable moment.
A developer may schedule a timer to read state after an update, expecting the interface to have settled. Meanwhile, promise callbacks trigger additional state changes before the timer runs.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A retry system may use timers while authentication refresh logic uses promises. The refresh completes, queues follow-up work, and changes shared state before the retry callback gets a chance to execute.
The problem becomes even more visible when the main thread is busy.
A timer scheduled for 100 milliseconds is not guaranteed to run after exactly 100 milliseconds. It can only run after the delay has passed and the runtime is free to execute it.
If JavaScript is parsing a large response, rendering an expensive component, processing a huge array, or running a synchronous calculation, the timer waits.
That is why a supposedly precise loading delay, animation step, or inactivity timeout may fire much later than expected.
Timers express minimum delays, not appointments.
Promises express future completion, not immediate continuation.
The event loop coordinates these behaviors according to defined rules, but the code becomes fragile when developers use scheduling mechanisms as substitutes for explicit workflow control.
A timer should not be used to “wait until the state is probably ready.”
The program should know which event, promise, render, or state transition proves that readiness.
Timing guesses eventually fail.
Callbacks Captured State That Was Already Old
Not every event loop bug is about queue order.
Some are about what a callback remembers.
Closures allow callbacks to retain access to variables from the scope where they were created. This is useful, but it can become dangerous in applications where state changes while asynchronous work remains pending.
A callback may execute later while still reading an older value.
In a frontend framework, a timer or promise callback can capture state from a previous render. By the time it runs, the user may have changed the value several times.
The callback is not reading the current application state.
It is reading the state that existed when the callback was created.
This can produce bugs that seem inconsistent because the same function behaves differently depending on when it was registered.
A notification may display an old username. An autosave operation may send a previous version of the form. A delayed validation result may replace a newer error state. A retry callback may use an expired token even though the application has already refreshed it.
The callback is behaving correctly according to its closure.
The developer assumed it would consult the latest state.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

These bugs become particularly confusing when logs are added after the fact. Logging the current state during a render shows the newest value. Logging inside the delayed callback shows the captured value. Both logs are accurate, but they describe different points in time.
The fix depends on the intended ownership.
Sometimes the callback should use the value from the moment the operation began. An upload request should generally remain associated with the file the user originally selected.
Sometimes it should consult the latest value. A timer that performs inactivity cleanup may need current session state rather than the state from when the timer was scheduled.
The mistake is allowing that choice to happen accidentally.
Reliable asynchronous code makes the time boundary visible.
Is this callback operating on a snapshot, or should it read live state? Can its result still be applied? Has the object it references been replaced? Does the component or workflow that created it still exist?
A callback running later does not automatically become part of the current application moment.
It arrives from the past carrying whatever state the code allowed it to keep.
Repeated Actions Created Overlapping Timelines
Many asynchronous flows are designed as if a user action can happen only once.
The user clicks save. The application starts a request. The request finishes. The interface updates.
That is the clean timeline.
Real users double-click. They retry when feedback is slow. They navigate away and return. They submit another action before the first one completes. They open the same workflow in two tabs.
Each action creates another valid timeline.
If the code does not define how those timelines interact, timing decides.
A double-click can create two purchases, two records, or two background jobs. Disabling the button helps, but the state update that disables it may not happen before the second click event is accepted.
A retry can duplicate a side effect if the first request succeeded but its response was lost. The frontend sees a timeout and assumes nothing happened. The server has already completed the operation.
A user can navigate away while a request is pending. The callback later
